narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—landing at a 40.9% chance of not passing. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a story: the strongest thread pulling toward that tougher outcome was your Year 3 decile of 2, placing you in the lower ranks among your peers at that crucial later stage, which often signals challenges in building the momentum needed for bar success. Layered on top was your LSAT score of 32, which, while respectable, fell short of the benchmarks that typically light the way for smoother passage. Your Year 1 decile of 2 echoed that early pattern, suggesting consistency in a competitive field where top performers pull ahead. Even with your upper-middle family income offering some stability, it didn't quite offset those academic signals in the model's view. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 added another nudge in the same direction, painting a picture of steady but not standout preparation from your undergrad days. As a white male studying full-time, those traits fit patterns we've seen before that, combined with the rest, tipped the scales toward caution rather than confidence. It's not that any one piece defined you—it's this blend that made the model pause, whispering that the road ahead might demand more than most to conquer the bar. This isn't the end of your story, though; many have rewritten similar chapters by targeting those deciles and scores head-on. What if you dove deeper into LSAT prep or strategies to climb those school rankings? You've got the drive—let's turn this into your plot twist. With encouragement, [Your Guide].
